EDWARD E. TUTTLE                    ALAN E. FRIEDMAN              T U T T L E & T A Y L O R                EDWARD W. TUTTLE (1877-1960)
WILLIAM T. TAYLOR                JAMES MARTIN FRASER
ROBERT H. TAYLOR                 RICHARD B. LEVINE
MERLIN N. CALL                   HAROLD J. KWALWASSER                          INCORPORATED
JULIAN SPERLING                  J. DEAN HELLER
HOWARD E. FREEMAN                ROBERT L. STEINBERG              ATTORNEYS AT LAW              THOMAS A. REYNOLDS
C. GRAHAM TEBBE, JR.             TIMI ANYON GALLEN                                              OF COUNSEL
JOSEPH M. LYNCH                  ANDREA M. CONCORAN               609 SOUTH GRAND AVENUE
LELAND NELSON                    JAMES R. MOULTON
A. JAMES ROBERTS III             MARILYN CLARE                    LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017            WASHINGTON OFFICE
RAYMOND C. FISHER                CHARLES L. WILTMANN                                            1730 K STREET, N.W.
BURTON I. GORDON                 WILLIAM T. TAYLOR, JR.           (213) 683-0600               WASHINGTON, D.C. 20006
W. HUNT DALLAS                   DAVID E. ALTSCHUL                                              (202) 785-0460
DAVID E. BEAN                    EDWARD N. ROBINSON
MICHAEL GLAZER                   JAMES H. WEBER
JOSEPH N. AUSTIN                 MARJORIE S. STEINBERG
ERIC J. JONNET                   DOUGLAS W. BECK
JEFFREY L. GRAHAM


October 5, 1976


Ms. Nan Hamilton
Technical Assistance and Analysis Unit
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Ms. Hamilton:

     Last Thursday, September 30, I described to you
the situation of a client of this firm who has personally
made approximately $4,950 of gifts to candidates for state
and local office in the State of California. The client's
wife has made an additional several hundred dollars of such
gifts, such that the combined total of their gifts to can-
didates for state and local offices during 1976 is in excess
of $5,000. The client made gifts in excess of $5,000 in
1974, made the appropriate disclosures for 1974, but made
less than $5,000 of contributions in 1975.

     The gifts made by the client's wife were made
entirely of her own volition and were made by checks drawn
on a separate account in her name. Her account is subject
to the husband's signature authority and is funded with
community property.

     Based on this set of facts, you advised me that
gifts made by the wife would not be aggregated with gifts
made by the husband for purposes of determining whether
total gifts of the husband were over the $5,000 "major
contributor" limit requiring the filing of campaign contri-
bution statements for 1975 and 1976.

     If I have incorrectly summarized our conversation,
or if you have any reason to understand that the foregoing

This advice is incorrect. See  A-84-015

TUTTLE & TAYLOR
INCORPORATED


Ms. Nan Hamilton
Page 2
October 5, 1976


summary of the applicable rules is inaccurate, I would
appreciate it if you could so advise me.

Sincerely yours,

TUTTLE & TAYLOR

By C. David Anderson
C. David Anderson

CDA/am

TUTTLE & TAYLOR

embedding.first_500_words: This letter is from Tuttle & Taylor, Attorneys at Law, dated October 5, 1976. The firm is writing to Ms. Nan Hamilton of the Fair Political Practices Commission regarding a client who has made gifts to candidates for state and local office in California. The client's wife has also made additional gifts, bringing the total contributions to over $5,000. The firm advises that gifts made by the wife would not be aggregated with those of the husband for determining whether the husband exceeded the $5,000 "major contributor" limit requiring campaign contribution statements. The letter states: Ms. Nan Hamilton Technical Assistance and Analysis Unit Fair Political Practices Commission 1100 "K" Street Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Ms. Hamilton: Last Thursday, September 30, I described to you the situation of a client of this firm who has personally made approximately $4,950 of gifts to candidates for state and local office in the State of California. The client's wife has made an additional several hundred dollars of such gifts, such that the combined total of their gifts to candidates for state and local offices during 1976 is in excess of $5,000. The client made gifts in excess of $5,000 in 1974, made the appropriate disclosures for 1974, but made less than $5,000 of contributions in 1975. The gifts made by the client's wife were made entirely of her own volition and were made by checks drawn on a separate account in her name. Her account is subject to the husband's signature authority and is funded with community property. Based on this set of facts, you advised me that gifts made by the wife would not be aggregated with gifts made by the husband for purposes of determining whether total gifts of the husband were over the $5,000 "major contributor" limit requiring the filing of campaign contribution statements for 1975 and 1976. If I have incorrectly summarized our conversation, or if you have any reason to understand that the foregoing Ms. Nan Hamilton Page 2 October 5, 1976 summary of the applicable rules is inaccurate, I would appreciate it if you could so advise me. Sincerely yours, TUTTLE & TAYLOR By C. David Anderson CDA/am The image you provided is completely blank, with no visible text, numbers, or any other content. It appears to be a white page with two holes at the top, possibly from a binder. If you have another page or section of the document that you would like me to help with, please provide it.
